message.content:
Reviewing task **T113** (goals:G6), ROUND 2, worktree `/home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-af4b721425a379dd8` (branch t113-fix, HEAD 7c46f43, base 39d0cb1). DOCS-ONLY (docs/install.md §9 + §5 C6). You filed 3 round-1 criticisms; review `git diff 39d0cb1..HEAD` and confirm each resolved:
1. §9 intro bold /0 warning reworded to the actual daemon-splits-at-UAPI invariant (config-level 0.0.0.0/0 safe; engine-boundary is the constraint), no longer contradicting §9.1's TOML or §3. Verify against splitDefaultRoute (internal/device/device.go).
2. §9.2 widen-allowed_ips alternative now states it additionally requires widening concentrator MASQUERADE -s to cover the client subnet, flagged unvalidated. Trace it: does the added guidance actually make the widen branch work end-to-end?
3. §9.2 SNAT-address pointer now references the edge's §4 Address= (10.77.0.2), not the peer allowed_ips (concentrator's 10.77.0.1).

Be adversarial: re-trace both data-path branches for consistency; confirm no new contradiction and the SNAT primary path is intact. Run the FULL gate `nix develop -c sh -c 'go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...'`. Return the structured verdict JSON {taskId, verdict, criticism[], questions[], defects[], rationale, summary}. Write NOTHING to the ledger.

attachment.content:
- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
diff --git a/docs/install.md b/docs/install.md
index 35adc37..9de8872 100644
--- a/docs/install.md
+++ b/docs/install.md
@@ -1117,11 +1117,14 @@ WAN, each pinned by `source_addr`) and a client LAN on a third VLAN;
 concentrator = a public host reached over NAT. Substitute your own
 addresses/interfaces throughout.
 
-**Never write a literal `allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]` (D35, open: it wedges
-the WG handshake permanently — see §3's cross-field constraints).** Both
-routes below reach the same result — an edge that can route to the whole
-internet through the tunnel — without ever handing the engine a literal
-`/0`:
+**A config-level literal `allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]` is safe to write** —
+the daemon unconditionally splits it into the wg-quick `/1`+`/1` pair at UAPI
+render (§3's `splitDefaultRoute`), so the *engine* itself never receives the
+literal `/0` that would otherwise wedge the WG handshake permanently (D35,
+open: the underlying engine defect this daemon-side split works around).
+Both routes below reach the same result — an edge that can route to the
+whole internet through the tunnel — by relying on that daemon-guaranteed
+split, never a raw `/0` at the engine boundary:
 
 ### 9.1 Edge: install the default-route split (daemon-automated, §3)
 
@@ -1170,7 +1173,10 @@ sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -s 192.168.223.0/24 -o wanbond0 -j SNAT --to
 (`192.168.223.0/24` / table `223` / `10.77.0.2` are the production Pi's
 client-LAN VLAN, routing table, and tunnel address, per `wanbond-fixes.md`
 §C3 — substitute your own client subnet, an unused table number, and the
-edge's `allowed_ips` address from its own peer config, §3.)
+edge's own tunnel address from its interface `Address=` in §4. **Not** the
+`allowed_ips` in the edge's own peer config, §3 — that holds the
+*concentrator's* tunnel address as seen from the edge (e.g. `10.77.0.1/32`),
+not the edge's own.)
 
 **Alternative — widen the concentrator's `allowed_ips` instead of SNAT-ing on
 the edge:** set the concentrator's peer entry for this edge to
@@ -1179,6 +1185,17 @@ the edge:** set the concentrator's peer entry for this edge to
 now cryptokey-routes the client subnet directly to this peer) for one fewer
 operator-owned step; pick one form, not both.
 
+This branch changes what source address reaches the concentrator's WAN NIC:
+without the edge-side SNAT, forwarded packets keep their original
+client-subnet source (`192.168.223.0/24`), which the §9.3 / §5 C6
+`MASQUERADE -s <tunnel-net>` rule (scoped to the tunnel subnet, e.g.
+`10.77.0.0/24`) does **not** match — so this branch additionally requires
+widening that concentrator `MASQUERADE -s` to also cover the client subnet
+(or replacing it with a supernet covering both), otherwise the reply traffic
+leaves the WAN with an RFC1918 source and the return path dies. Unlike the
+SNAT branch above, this alternative has **not** been validated on the
+production Pi/o3 deploy.
+
 Put the `ip_forward` toggle, the `ip rule`/`ip route`, and (if used) the
 `iptables -t nat` SNAT rule into the edge's addressing oneshot
 (`wanbond-addressing@edge.service`, §4) — `wanbond0` and everything routed

content.content:
1095	by port`). This is a classification of the *port*, not the *wire format*: a
1096	random-payload UDP flow to `:51820` is labelled WireGuard, while the identical
1097	payload to a non-registered port is `Unknown`. wanbond's payload is verified
1098	indistinguishable from random by `TestWireFormatAudit` (T26) and by
1099	`TestP5DPI`, which reads nDPI's per-flow `Confidence` and only treats a
1100	**payload/content** match (`Confidence: DPI`) as a classification — a port
1101	guess is disregarded.
1102	
1103	Deployment consequence: `wireguard.listen_port` is operator-configurable, so on
1104	a hostile network that classifies by port, **prefer a non-registered UDP port**
1105	(any high, unassigned port) for the concentrator's `listen_port` and the edge's
1106	`endpoint`. This avoids the trivial port-based "VPN" label. It is a deployment
1107	consideration, not a payload weakness — the obfuscated payload itself does not
1108	identify the tunnel as WireGuard/VPN.
1109	
1110	## 9. Full-tunnel / client-LAN recipe (C3)
1111	
1112	wanbond's primary use case: route an entire client LAN's traffic through the
1113	bonded tunnel and out the concentrator's public IP. This is end-to-end
1114	validated on the production Pi/o3 deploy (`wanbond-fixes.md`): edge = a
1115	Raspberry Pi with two WAN uplinks arriving as VLAN sub-interfaces (one per
1116	WAN, each pinned by `source_addr`) and a client LAN on a third VLAN;
1117	concentrator = a public host reached over NAT. Substitute your own
1118	addresses/interfaces throughout.
1119	
1120	**A config-level literal `allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]` is safe to write** —
1121	the daemon unconditionally splits it into the wg-quick `/1`+`/1` pair at UAPI
1122	render (§3's `splitDefaultRoute`), so the *engine* itself never receives the
1123	literal `/0` that would otherwise wedge the WG handshake permanently (D35,
1124	open: the underlying engine defect this daemon-side split works around).
1125	Both routes below reach the same result — an edge that can route to the
1126	whole internet through the tunnel — by relying on that daemon-guaranteed
1127	split, never a raw `/0` at the engine boundary:
1128	
1129	### 9.1 Edge: install the default-route split (daemon-automated, §3)
1130	
1131	Set `mode = "default-route"` on the concentrator peer alongside a
1132	`0.0.0.0/0` (and, for IPv6, `::/0`) `allowed_ips` entry:
1133	
1134	```toml
1135	[[wireguard.peers]]
1136	public_key = "<base64 concentrator public key>"
1137	endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"
1138	allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
1139	mode = "default-route"             # see §3: edge-only, full-tunnel opt-in
1140	```
1141	
1142	The daemon splits `0.0.0.0/0` into the wg-quick `/1`+`/1` pair
1143	(`0.0.0.0/1` + `128.0.0.0/1`) at UAPI render regardless of `mode` — so the
1144	engine itself never sees the literal `/0` that wedges D35 — and, because
1145	`mode = "default-route"` is set, ALSO installs that same split as scope-link
1146	routes via `wanbond0` once the interface comes up, withdrawing them on stop.
1147	This is the daemon's **only** route programming anywhere in this recipe;
1148	everything below (9.2–9.3) is entirely operator-owned, same boundary as §3/§4.
1149	
1150	If you would rather not opt into `mode` at all, install the equivalent
1151	routes yourself instead (e.g. from the addressing oneshot, §4):
1152	
1153	```sh
1154	sudo ip route add 0.0.0.0/1 dev wanbond0
1155	sudo ip route add 128.0.0.0/1 dev wanbond0
1156	```
1157	
1158	### 9.2 Edge: route the client LAN onto wanbond0 (operator-owned)
1159	
1160	Policy-route the client subnet to `wanbond0` and **SNAT to the edge's own
1161	tunnel address**, so the concentrator's per-edge `allowed_ips =
1162	<edge-tunnel-ip>/32` (the ordinary point-to-point form, §3) still matches
1163	the source address of forwarded traffic — no widening needed on the
1164	concentrator side:
1165	
1166	```sh
1167	sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
1168	sudo ip rule add from 192.168.223.0/24 lookup 223
1169	sudo ip route add default dev wanbond0 table 223
1170	sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -s 192.168.223.0/24 -o wanbond0 -j SNAT --to-source 10.77.0.2
1171	```
1172	
1173	(`192.168.223.0/24` / table `223` / `10.77.0.2` are the production Pi's
1174	client-LAN VLAN, routing table, and tunnel address, per `wanbond-fixes.md`
1175	§C3 — substitute your own client subnet, an unused table number, and the
1176	edge's own tunnel address from its interface `Address=` in §4. **Not** the
1177	`allowed_ips` in the edge's own peer config, §3 — that holds the
1178	*concentrator's* tunnel address as seen from the edge (e.g. `10.77.0.1/32`),
1179	not the edge's own.)
1180	
1181	**Alternative — widen the concentrator's `allowed_ips` instead of SNAT-ing on
1182	the edge:** set the concentrator's peer entry for this edge to
1183	`allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.2/32", "192.168.223.0/24"]` and skip the edge-side
1184	`iptables -t nat` step above. Trades a wider `allowed_ips` (the concentrator
1185	now cryptokey-routes the client subnet directly to this peer) for one fewer
1186	operator-owned step; pick one form, not both.
1187	
1188	This branch changes what source address reaches the concentrator's WAN NIC:
1189	without the edge-side SNAT, forwarded packets keep their original
1190	client-subnet source (`192.168.223.0/24`), which the §9.3 / §5 C6
1191	`MASQUERADE -s <tunnel-net>` rule (scoped to the tunnel subnet, e.g.
1192	`10.77.0.0/24`) does **not** match — so this branch additionally requires
1193	widening that concentrator `MASQUERADE -s` to also cover the client subnet
1194	(or replacing it with a supernet covering both), otherwise the reply traffic
1195	leaves the WAN with an RFC1918 source and the return path dies. Unlike the
1196	SNAT branch above, this alternative has **not** been validated on the
1197	production Pi/o3 deploy.
1198	
1199	Put the `ip_forward` toggle, the `ip rule`/`ip route`, and (if used) the
1200	`iptables -t nat` SNAT rule into the edge's addressing oneshot
1201	(`wanbond-addressing@edge.service`, §4) — `wanbond0` and everything routed
1202	through it are torn down and recreated on every daemon restart unless
1203	`tun_persist = true` (§4, I7), so these must be re-applied the same way the
1204	existing persistence recipe re-applies addressing.
1205	
1206	### 9.3 Concentrator: NAT and forward the tunnel traffic out the WAN (operator-owned)
1207	
1208	See §5 "Concentrator: NAT/forwarding prerequisites for routed traffic (C6)"
1209	— `ip_forward`, `MASQUERADE`, and the `FORWARD` established/related accept
1210	are all required for this recipe and, like 9.2, are entirely
1211	operator-owned: the daemon programs none of them (§3's `mode` boundary
1212	covers routes only, never NAT/forwarding/policy-routing).
1213	
1214	### 9.4 Persistence
1215	
1216	Both the edge's policy-route/SNAT rules (9.2) and the concentrator's
1217	NAT/forward rules (§5 C6) reference `wanbond0` or its addresses and so must
1218	survive both reboots and daemon restarts. Use the existing §4
1219	`wanbond-addressing@<role>.service` oneshot for anything keyed to the
1220	interface (9.2's `ip rule`/`ip route`, and the `iptables -t nat` SNAT rule
1221	if you took the SNAT branch above), and `netfilter-persistent save` (§5) for
1222	the concentrator's `iptables`/`ip6tables` rules, which persist independently
1223	of the interface. `net.ipv4.ip_forward` persists on its own once written to
1224	`/etc/sysctl.d/` (§5 C6).
1225

file.content:
by port`). This is a classification of the *port*, not the *wire format*: a
random-payload UDP flow to `:51820` is labelled WireGuard, while the identical
payload to a non-registered port is `Unknown`. wanbond's payload is verified
indistinguishable from random by `TestWireFormatAudit` (T26) and by
`TestP5DPI`, which reads nDPI's per-flow `Confidence` and only treats a
**payload/content** match (`Confidence: DPI`) as a classification — a port
guess is disregarded.

Deployment consequence: `wireguard.listen_port` is operator-configurable, so on
a hostile network that classifies by port, **prefer a non-registered UDP port**
(any high, unassigned port) for the concentrator's `listen_port` and the edge's
`endpoint`. This avoids the trivial port-based "VPN" label. It is a deployment
consideration, not a payload weakness — the obfuscated payload itself does not
identify the tunnel as WireGuard/VPN.

## 9. Full-tunnel / client-LAN recipe (C3)

wanbond's primary use case: route an entire client LAN's traffic through the
bonded tunnel and out the concentrator's public IP. This is end-to-end
validated on the production Pi/o3 deploy (`wanbond-fixes.md`): edge = a
Raspberry Pi with two WAN uplinks arriving as VLAN sub-interfaces (one per
WAN, each pinned by `source_addr`) and a client LAN on a third VLAN;
concentrator = a public host reached over NAT. Substitute your own
addresses/interfaces throughout.

**A config-level literal `allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]` is safe to write** —
the daemon unconditionally splits it into the wg-quick `/1`+`/1` pair at UAPI
render (§3's `splitDefaultRoute`), so the *engine* itself never receives the
literal `/0` that would otherwise wedge the WG handshake permanently (D35,
open: the underlying engine defect this daemon-side split works around).
Both routes below reach the same result — an edge that can route to the
whole internet through the tunnel — by relying on that daemon-guaranteed
split, never a raw `/0` at the engine boundary:

### 9.1 Edge: install the default-route split (daemon-automated, §3)

Set `mode = "default-route"` on the concentrator peer alongside a
`0.0.0.0/0` (and, for IPv6, `::/0`) `allowed_ips` entry:

```toml
[[wireguard.peers]]
public_key = "<base64 concentrator public key>"
endpoint = "203.0.113.7:51820"
allowed_ips = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
mode = "default-route"             # see §3: edge-only, full-tunnel opt-in
```

The daemon splits `0.0.0.0/0` into the wg-quick `/1`+`/1` pair
(`0.0.0.0/1` + `128.0.0.0/1`) at UAPI render regardless of `mode` — so the
engine itself never sees the literal `/0` that wedges D35 — and, because
`mode = "default-route"` is set, ALSO installs that same split as scope-link
routes via `wanbond0` once the interface comes up, withdrawing them on stop.
This is the daemon's **only** route programming anywhere in this recipe;
everything below (9.2–9.3) is entirely operator-owned, same boundary as §3/§4.

If you would rather not opt into `mode` at all, install the equivalent
routes yourself instead (e.g. from the addressing oneshot, §4):

```sh
sudo ip route add 0.0.0.0/1 dev wanbond0
sudo ip route add 128.0.0.0/1 dev wanbond0
```

### 9.2 Edge: route the client LAN onto wanbond0 (operator-owned)

Policy-route the client subnet to `wanbond0` and **SNAT to the edge's own
tunnel address**, so the concentrator's per-edge `allowed_ips =
<edge-tunnel-ip>/32` (the ordinary point-to-point form, §3) still matches
the source address of forwarded traffic — no widening needed on the
concentrator side:

```sh
sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
sudo ip rule add from 192.168.223.0/24 lookup 223
sudo ip route add default dev wanbond0 table 223
sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -s 192.168.223.0/24 -o wanbond0 -j SNAT --to-source 10.77.0.2
```

(`192.168.223.0/24` / table `223` / `10.77.0.2` are the production Pi's
client-LAN VLAN, routing table, and tunnel address, per `wanbond-fixes.md`
§C3 — substitute your own client subnet, an unused table number, and the
edge's own tunnel address from its interface `Address=` in §4. **Not** the
`allowed_ips` in the edge's own peer config, §3 — that holds the
*concentrator's* tunnel address as seen from the edge (e.g. `10.77.0.1/32`),
not the edge's own.)

**Alternative — widen the concentrator's `allowed_ips` instead of SNAT-ing on
the edge:** set the concentrator's peer entry for this edge to
`allowed_ips = ["10.77.0.2/32", "192.168.223.0/24"]` and skip the edge-side
`iptables -t nat` step above. Trades a wider `allowed_ips` (the concentrator
now cryptokey-routes the client subnet directly to this peer) for one fewer
operator-owned step; pick one form, not both.

This branch changes what source address reaches the concentrator's WAN NIC:
without the edge-side SNAT, forwarded packets keep their original
client-subnet source (`192.168.223.0/24`), which the §9.3 / §5 C6
`MASQUERADE -s <tunnel-net>` rule (scoped to the tunnel subnet, e.g.
`10.77.0.0/24`) does **not** match — so this branch additionally requires
widening that concentrator `MASQUERADE -s` to also cover the client subnet
(or replacing it with a supernet covering both), otherwise the reply traffic
leaves the WAN with an RFC1918 source and the return path dies. Unlike the
SNAT branch above, this alternative has **not** been validated on the
production Pi/o3 deploy.

Put the `ip_forward` toggle, the `ip rule`/`ip route`, and (if used) the
`iptables -t nat` SNAT rule into the edge's addressing oneshot
(`wanbond-addressing@edge.service`, §4) — `wanbond0` and everything routed
through it are torn down and recreated on every daemon restart unless
`tun_persist = true` (§4, I7), so these must be re-applied the same way the
existing persistence recipe re-applies addressing.

### 9.3 Concentrator: NAT and forward the tunnel traffic out the WAN (operator-owned)

See §5 "Concentrator: NAT/forwarding prerequisites for routed traffic (C6)"
— `ip_forward`, `MASQUERADE`, and the `FORWARD` established/related accept
are all required for this recipe and, like 9.2, are entirely
operator-owned: the daemon programs none of them (§3's `mode` boundary
covers routes only, never NAT/forwarding/policy-routing).

### 9.4 Persistence

Both the edge's policy-route/SNAT rules (9.2) and the concentrator's
NAT/forward rules (§5 C6) reference `wanbond0` or its addresses and so must
survive both reboots and daemon restarts. Use the existing §4
`wanbond-addressing@<role>.service` oneshot for anything keyed to the
interface (9.2's `ip rule`/`ip route`, and the `iptables -t nat` SNAT rule
if you took the SNAT branch above), and `netfilter-persistent save` (§5) for
the concentrator's `iptables`/`ip6tables` rules, which persist independently
of the interface. `net.ipv4.ip_forward` persists on its own once written to
`/etc/sysctl.d/` (§5 C6).

980	### Concentrator: NAT/forwarding prerequisites for routed traffic (C6)
981	
982	Required **only** when the concentrator routes traffic on beyond itself — any
983	full-tunnel / client-LAN recipe (§9). Skip this subsection for a plain
984	point-to-point tunnel that never carries traffic addressed past the tunnel
985	endpoints themselves. All three items are **operator-owned** — the daemon
986	programs none of `ip_forward`, NAT, or firewall rules — and match the
987	production Pi/o3 deploy (`wanbond-fixes.md` §C3/C6).
988	
989	1. **`ip_forward` — REQUIRED.** Off by default on most distros; without it the
990	   kernel never routes tunnel packets on to the WAN, full stop:
991	
992	   ```sh
993	   sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
994	   echo 'net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1' | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/99-wanbond-forward.conf
995	   ```
996	
997	2. **`MASQUERADE` the tunnel network out the WAN interface — REQUIRED.**
998	   `<tunnel-net>` is the inner WireGuard subnet (e.g. `10.77.0.0/24`);
999	   `<wan>` is the concentrator's actual internet-facing NIC — **not**
1000	   `wanbond0` (on o3 this is the private `enp0s6` that OCI itself NATs to the
1001	   public IP; substitute your own WAN-facing interface):
1002	
1003	   ```sh
1004	   sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -s <tunnel-net> -o <wan> -j MASQUERADE
1005	   ```
1006	
1007	3. **`FORWARD`: accept both directions — REQUIRED, easy to miss.** The
1008	   "Concentrator: tunnel-interface ACCEPT" rule above (`-i wanbond0 ACCEPT`)
1009	   is an **`INPUT`** rule; it only covers traffic *terminating on* the
1010	   concentrator and says nothing about `FORWARD`, which is what packets pass
1011	   through when the concentrator merely routes them on to the WAN and back.
1012	   The default `FORWARD` policy is `REJECT`/`DROP` on the same distros/clouds
1013	   called out above (OCI included), so it silently drops one direction unless
1014	   both of these are present:
1015	
1016	   ```sh
1017	   # outbound leg: client-subnet traffic entering FORWARD via wanbond0
1018	   sudo iptables -I FORWARD -i wanbond0 -j ACCEPT
1019	   # return leg: the outbound-leg ACCEPT above covers only that ONE
1020	   # direction — without this conntrack-ESTABLISHED accept too, the default
1021	   # FORWARD REJECT/DROP silently drops all RETURN traffic and the tunnel
1022	   # looks one-way-dead from the client's perspective
1023	   sudo iptables -I FORWARD -o wanbond0 -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
1024	   ```
1025	
1026	**Persistence.** All of the above (the `sysctl.d` drop-in aside) are
1027	runtime-only mutations, same as the tunnel-interface ACCEPT above — persist
1028	them with the identical `netfilter-persistent` flow already covered in
1029	"Concentrator: persist the rules across reboots" (this section):
1030	
1031	```sh
1032	sudo netfilter-persistent save
1033	```
1034	
1035	`net.ipv4.ip_forward` written to `/etc/sysctl.d/` (step 1) is already
1036	persistent across reboots without further action (`sysctl --system` re-reads
1037	`/etc/sysctl.d/` on boot).
1038	
1039	### Edge

file.content:
### Concentrator: NAT/forwarding prerequisites for routed traffic (C6)

Required **only** when the concentrator routes traffic on beyond itself — any
full-tunnel / client-LAN recipe (§9). Skip this subsection for a plain
point-to-point tunnel that never carries traffic addressed past the tunnel
endpoints themselves. All three items are **operator-owned** — the daemon
programs none of `ip_forward`, NAT, or firewall rules — and match the
production Pi/o3 deploy (`wanbond-fixes.md` §C3/C6).

1. **`ip_forward` — REQUIRED.** Off by default on most distros; without it the
   kernel never routes tunnel packets on to the WAN, full stop:

   ```sh
   sudo sysctl -w net.ipv4.ip_forward=1
   echo 'net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1' | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/99-wanbond-forward.conf
   ```

2. **`MASQUERADE` the tunnel network out the WAN interface — REQUIRED.**
   `<tunnel-net>` is the inner WireGuard subnet (e.g. `10.77.0.0/24`);
   `<wan>` is the concentrator's actual internet-facing NIC — **not**
   `wanbond0` (on o3 this is the private `enp0s6` that OCI itself NATs to the
   public IP; substitute your own WAN-facing interface):

   ```sh
   sudo iptables -t nat -A POSTROUTING -s <tunnel-net> -o <wan> -j MASQUERADE
   ```

3. **`FORWARD`: accept both directions — REQUIRED, easy to miss.** The
   "Concentrator: tunnel-interface ACCEPT" rule above (`-i wanbond0 ACCEPT`)
   is an **`INPUT`** rule; it only covers traffic *terminating on* the
   concentrator and says nothing about `FORWARD`, which is what packets pass
   through when the concentrator merely routes them on to the WAN and back.
   The default `FORWARD` policy is `REJECT`/`DROP` on the same distros/clouds
   called out above (OCI included), so it silently drops one direction unless
   both of these are present:

   ```sh
   # outbound leg: client-subnet traffic entering FORWARD via wanbond0
   sudo iptables -I FORWARD -i wanbond0 -j ACCEPT
   # return leg: the outbound-leg ACCEPT above covers only that ONE
   # direction — without this conntrack-ESTABLISHED accept too, the default
   # FORWARD REJECT/DROP silently drops all RETURN traffic and the tunnel
   # looks one-way-dead from the client's perspective
   sudo iptables -I FORWARD -o wanbond0 -m conntrack --ctstate ESTABLISHED,RELATED -j ACCEPT
   ```

**Persistence.** All of the above (the `sysctl.d` drop-in aside) are
runtime-only mutations, same as the tunnel-interface ACCEPT above — persist
them with the identical `netfilter-persistent` flow already covered in
"Concentrator: persist the rules across reboots" (this section):

```sh
sudo netfilter-persistent save
```

`net.ipv4.ip_forward` written to `/etc/sysctl.d/` (step 1) is already
persistent across reboots without further action (`sysctl --system` re-reads
`/etc/sysctl.d/` on boot).

### Edge

content.text:
The gate is green and all three round-1 criticisms were addressed as written — but the adversarial end-to-end trace of the widen branch shows the round-1 fix for criticism #2 is incomplete.

**Round-1 resolution check:**

1. **§9 intro** — resolved. Now states config-level `/0` is safe and the daemon unconditionally splits at UAPI render. Verified against `uapiConfig`/`splitDefaultRoute` (`internal/device/device.go:1055-1080`): the split happens for every peer regardless of `mode`, exactly as claimed. No remaining contradiction with §9.1's TOML or §3 (lines 701-709).
2. **§9.2 widen alternative** — the added MASQUERADE-widening paragraph is correct and necessary (C6's rule at install.md:1004 is indeed `-s <tunnel-net>`), and it is flagged unvalidated. But it is **not sufficient** — see criticism below.
3. **SNAT pointer** — resolved. Points to §4 `Address=` (line 824: `Address=10.77.0.2/24`, edge), matching the SNAT `--to-source 10.77.0.2`.

**Widen-branch end-to-end trace (the remaining defect):** outbound works (cryptokey src check passes with widened `allowed_ips`; C6 FORWARD rules are interface-scoped, not source-scoped; widened MASQUERADE NATs out the WAN). Return path: reply hits the WAN IP, conntrack de-NATs to dst `192.168.223.x` — and the concentrator kernel has **no route** for `192.168.223.0/24` toward `wanbond0`. The daemon's only route programming is `installRoutes(defaultRoutePrefixes(...))` (device.go:366-368), non-empty only for `mode = "default-route"`, which config validation rejects on the concentrator (config.go:1121-1122); §4's networkd file provides only the on-link `10.77.0.0/24`. Cryptokey routing selects a peer only for packets already routed into the TUN — it installs nothing. So the de-NATed reply follows the concentrator's default route out the WAN and dies. The widen branch as documented still fails end-to-end.

### Session summary
- **Did:** reviewed task T113 round 2 (diff 39d0cb1..7c46f43, docs/install.md only)
- **Achieved:** verdict disapprove; 3 criticisms / 0 questions
- **Discovered:** round-1 items 1 and 3 fully resolved and verified against device.go/§4/§5-C6; item 2's MASQUERADE guidance is correct but the widen branch still lacks the concentrator kernel route for the client subnet, so it remains non-functional end-to-end; full gate green (build/vet/test all pass)
- **Issues:** none

```json
{
  "taskId": "T113",
  "verdict": "disapprove",
  "criticism": [
    "§9.2 widen-allowed_ips branch is still not functional end-to-end: after the newly documented MASQUERADE -s widening, the de-NATed return packet (dst 192.168.223.x) has no concentrator kernel route toward wanbond0 — the daemon installs routes only for mode=default-route peers (device.go:366-368, defaultRoutePrefixes), that mode is rejected on the concentrator (config.go:1121-1122), and §4's networkd Address=10.77.0.1/24 covers only the tunnel /24; cryptokey allowed_ips installs no kernel routes. The paragraph must additionally require an operator-owned `ip route add <client-subnet> dev wanbond0` on the concentrator (plus its persistence, interface-keyed like §9.4's edge rules), or the return traffic exits the WAN with an unroutable RFC1918 destination.",
    "§9.2 new paragraph mislabels the failure direction: 'the reply traffic leaves the WAN with an RFC1918 source' — it is the forwarded client-outbound traffic that leaves the WAN un-NATed with an RFC1918 source; the replies never arrive. Reword to name the outbound leg.",
    "§9.2 new SNAT-pointer parenthetical states the edge's own peer-config allowed_ips 'holds the concentrator's tunnel address (e.g. 10.77.0.1/32)' — true for §3's point-to-point form it cites, but false for the config the §9 reader just wrote in §9.1, where it holds 0.0.0.0/0. Qualify it (point-to-point form holds the concentrator's /32; this recipe holds /0 — in neither case the edge's own address)."
  ],
  "questions": [],
  "defects": [],
  "rationale": "Round-1 items 1 and 3 verified resolved against splitDefaultRoute and §4 Address=; gate green. But the widen-branch fix is incomplete: tracing the return path shows the concentrator lacks a kernel route for the client subnet (daemon installs none on the concentrator role), so the documented alternative still fails end-to-end.",
  "summary": "Disapprove round 2: intro and SNAT-pointer fixes verified; widen branch still omits the required concentrator kernel route for the client subnet, plus two wording inaccuracies in the new text."
}
```
